parts.purpose: Hook — redox reactions power batteries, rust metals, and drive all energy metabolism

blocks.body: Acid-base reactions transfer protons (H⁺). Oxidation-reduction reactions transfer ELECTRONS. This electron transfer is what powers every battery, corrodes every bridge, fuels every cell in your body, and drives photosynthesis.

Redox (reduction-oxidation) reactions involve the transfer of electrons from one substance to another. One substance LOSES electrons (oxidation) while another GAINS electrons (reduction). They always happen together — you can't have one without the other.

When iron rusts, iron atoms lose electrons to oxygen. When a battery powers your phone, zinc loses electrons that flow through the circuit to copper. When you eat food, glucose loses electrons that are used to make ATP (energy). All redox.

blocks.title: See Redox Happen
blocks.body: Drop a clean iron nail into blue copper sulfate (CuSO₄) solution. Over 30 minutes, the blue fades and a copper-colored coating appears on the nail. Iron atoms on the surface lost electrons to Cu²⁺ ions in solution. Iron was oxidized; copper was reduced. You watched electron transfer in real time.

Another example: cut an apple and leave it exposed. The browning is oxidation — iron-containing enzymes in the apple react with oxygen in the air, transferring electrons. Squeeze lemon juice on the cut surface to slow it — the citric acid acts as an antioxidant, blocking the electron transfer.
Every time you see rust, tarnish, browning fruit, fire, or a working battery — you're seeing electron transfer. Redox reactions are the most common reaction type in nature.
Acid-base: H⁺ changes hands. Redox: electrons change hands. Chemistry is basically a never-ending game of hot potato with subatomic particles.

Your phone battery works because lithium atoms at the anode are SO eager to lose electrons that they push them through the entire circuit (powering your screen, processor, everything) to reach the cathode. Your phone runs on atomic-level generosity. When the lithium stops giving, your battery dies.
Iron rusting is literally iron atoms giving electrons to oxygen and slowly crumbling. Your car is committing molecular charity to the atmosphere. Oxidation is generosity that nobody asked for.

parts.purpose: Core — define oxidation (electron loss) and reduction (electron gain), the mnemonic, agents

blocks.title: OIL RIG
blocks.body: Oxidation Is Loss (of electrons). Reduction Is Gain (of electrons). The most famous mnemonic in chemistry. OIL RIG. Memorize it. Tattoo it. It solves half of redox.

items.label: Oxidation
items.body: Loss of electrons. The substance that is oxidized becomes MORE POSITIVE (loses negative charges). Example: Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻. Iron lost 2 electrons — it was oxidized.
items.examples: Fe → Fe²⁺, Zn → Zn²⁺, Na → Na⁺

items.label: Reduction
items.body: Gain of electrons. The substance that is reduced becomes MORE NEGATIVE (gains negative charges). Example: Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu. Copper gained 2 electrons — it was reduced.
items.examples: Cu²⁺ → Cu, O₂ → O²⁻, Cl₂ → Cl⁻

blocks.title: They Always Happen Together
blocks.body: If one substance loses electrons, another must gain them. Electrons don't just disappear. In Fe + CuSO₄ → FeSO₄ + Cu: iron is OXIDIZED (loses 2e⁻), copper is REDUCED (gains 2e⁻). The electrons flow from iron to copper.

blocks.title: Oxidizing and Reducing Agents
blocks.body: The oxidizing agent CAUSES oxidation by ACCEPTING electrons (it itself gets reduced). The reducing agent CAUSES reduction by DONATING electrons (it itself gets oxidized). Confusing? Think of it this way: the agent is named for what it DOES TO the other substance, not what happens to itself.
In Fe + CuSO₄: Iron is the reducing agent (it donates electrons, causing Cu²⁺ to be reduced). Cu²⁺ is the oxidizing agent (it accepts electrons, causing Fe to be oxidized).

blocks.title: Track the Electrons
blocks.body: In every redox reaction, follow the electrons. Write the two half-reactions separately.

items.label: Oxidation half-reaction
items.body: Na → Na⁺ + e⁻ (each Na loses 1 electron). Sodium is oxidized.

items.label: Reduction half-reaction
items.body: Cl₂ + 2e⁻ → 2Cl⁻ (each Cl gains 1 electron). Chlorine is reduced.

items.label: Combined
items.body: 2Na → 2Na⁺ + 2e⁻ AND Cl₂ + 2e⁻ → 2Cl⁻. Electrons lost = electrons gained. Balanced.

blocks.body: Half-reactions make redox clear: write the oxidation separately, the reduction separately, then verify that electrons lost = electrons gained. This is the same principle as balancing regular equations — conservation, but for electrons.

blocks.title: OIL RIG: The Only Mnemonic You Need
blocks.body: Oxidation Is Loss. Reduction Is Gain. OIL RIG. The name 'reduction' is confusing because gaining electrons seems like it should be called 'addition.' But historically, 'reduction' referred to metal ores being 'reduced' to pure metals (which involved gaining electrons). We're stuck with the name. OIL RIG saves you.

The 'oxidizing agent' gets reduced. The 'reducing agent' gets oxidized. Yes, this is deliberately confusing. The agent is named for what it does TO the other substance. An oxidizing agent causes oxidation (steals electrons) and is itself reduced. It's like calling a thief a 'gift-giving agent' because they give stolen goods to their accomplice. Chemistry naming is occasionally absurd.

Quick mental trick: the substance that LOSES electrons is the hero (giving to others) — the reducing agent. The substance that GAINS electrons is the villain (taking from others) — the oxidizing agent. Givers are reducers. Takers are oxidizers.

questions.question: In Fe + Cu²⁺ → Fe²⁺ + Cu, which substance is oxidized?

options.B: Fe — it loses electrons, going from Fe to Fe²⁺ (OIL: Oxidation Is Loss)
options.D: Neither — this isn't a redox reaction

questions.explanation: Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻. Iron LOST 2 electrons = oxidation (OIL). Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu. Copper GAINED 2 electrons = reduction (RIG). Iron is oxidized; copper is reduced.

questions.question: In the same reaction, which is the reducing agent?

options.A: Cu²⁺ — it caused iron to be reduced
options.B: Fe — it donated electrons, CAUSING Cu²⁺ to be reduced

questions.explanation: The reducing agent DONATES electrons (causing the other to be reduced). Fe gave its electrons to Cu²⁺, causing Cu²⁺ to gain electrons (be reduced). Fe is the reducing agent. Confusingly, the reducing agent itself gets oxidized.

parts.purpose: Teach oxidation number rules and how to use them to identify redox reactions

blocks.title: When It's Not Obvious
blocks.body: In 2Na + Cl₂ → 2NaCl, the electron transfer is clear (Na gives to Cl). But in many reactions, it's not obvious who lost and gained electrons. Oxidation numbers are a bookkeeping system that tracks electron ownership.

blocks.title: Oxidation Number (State)
blocks.body: A number assigned to each atom representing its 'effective charge' — how many electrons it has gained or lost relative to its neutral state. If an atom's oxidation number INCREASES in a reaction, it was oxidized. If it DECREASES, it was reduced.

items.label: Free elements = 0
items.body: Any element by itself: Na = 0, O₂ = 0, Fe = 0, Cl₂ = 0.

items.label: Sum of all oxidation numbers = compound's charge
items.body: Neutral compound: sum = 0. Polyatomic ion: sum = ion's charge.

blocks.title: Example: Is This Redox?
blocks.body: CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O. Assign oxidation numbers: In CH₄: H = +1 (rule 3), so C = −4 (since 4×(+1) + C = 0). In CO₂: O = −2 (rule 4), so C = +4 (since C + 2×(−2) = 0). Carbon went from −4 to +4: OXIDIZED (lost electrons). Oxygen went from 0 (O₂) to −2: REDUCED (gained electrons). This IS redox.

If ANY element changes oxidation number during a reaction, it's redox. If no oxidation numbers change, it's NOT redox. Neutralization (HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O) has no oxidation number changes — not redox. Combustion always involves oxidation number changes — always redox.

items.label: NaOH + HCl → NaCl + H₂O
items.hints: Na: +1 → +1. O: −2 → −2. H: +1 → +1. Cl: −1 → −1. NO change → NOT redox.

items.label: Fe₂O₃ + 3CO → 2Fe + 3CO₂
items.hints: Fe: +3 → 0 (reduced). C: +2 → +4 (oxidized). YES — redox. This is iron smelting!

items.label: CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂
items.hints: Ca: +2 → +2. C: +4 → +4. O: −2 → −2. NO change → NOT redox (decomposition only).

blocks.body: Quick test: if a free element appears on either side of the equation, it's almost certainly redox (something went from 0 to charged or vice versa).

blocks.title: Electron Accounting
blocks.body: Oxidation numbers are like bank account balances for electrons. Each atom starts with a balance (its oxidation number). If the balance goes UP (more positive) during a reaction, the atom LOST electrons — oxidized. If it goes DOWN (more negative), it GAINED electrons — reduced.

The rules are basically: elements alone = broke (0). Hydrogen = +1 (always lending). Oxygen = −2 (always borrowing). Everything else: do the math so the total adds up. It's like splitting a restaurant bill where hydrogen and oxygen always order the same thing.

Iron smelting (Fe₂O₃ + 3CO → 2Fe + 3CO₂) is a redox reaction humans discovered thousands of years ago. Iron ore (Fe³⁺) gets REDUCED to pure iron (Fe⁰) by carbon monoxide. The entire Iron Age was powered by a redox reaction nobody could explain until the 1800s. We built civilization on reactions we didn't understand.

questions.question: In 2Mg + O₂ → 2MgO, what are the oxidation number changes?

options.B: Both Mg and O are reduced

questions.explanation: Mg starts as free element (0), becomes Mg²⁺ in MgO (+2). Lost electrons → oxidized. O starts as O₂ (0), becomes O²⁻ in MgO (−2). Gained electrons → reduced.

questions.question: How can you quickly tell if a reaction is redox?

options.A: All reactions with oxygen are redox
options.B: Check if any element's oxidation number changes — if yes, it's redox
options.C: All reactions producing heat are redox
options.D: Only reactions with metals are redox

questions.explanation: Redox = electron transfer = oxidation number change. If every element keeps its oxidation number, no electrons transferred, not redox. Many O₂ reactions ARE redox, but not all (e.g., dissolving isn't). The oxidation number test is universal.

items.label: Batteries
items.body: A battery forces electrons to flow from oxidized anode to reduced cathode through an external circuit. That electron flow IS the electricity. Lithium-ion: Li oxidized at anode, CoO₂ reduced at cathode.

items.label: Corrosion (Rust)
items.body: 4Fe + 3O₂ + 6H₂O → 4Fe(OH)₃. Iron oxidized (0→+3), oxygen reduced (0→−2). Costs the US ~$276 billion/year in infrastructure damage.

items.label: Cellular Respiration
items.body: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O. Glucose oxidized (C: 0→+4), oxygen reduced (0→−2). This reaction powers every cell in your body.

items.label: Photosynthesis
items.body: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. The REVERSE of respiration. CO₂ reduced, water oxidized. Powered by sunlight. All food energy on Earth traces to this redox reaction.

items.label: Bleaching
items.body: Bleach (NaClO) is an oxidizing agent — it oxidizes the colored molecules in stains, breaking their bonds and removing the color.

blocks.body: Acid-base transfers H⁺. Redox transfers e⁻. Together, they explain most of the reactions in chemistry. Acid-base governs pH, digestion, and buffering. Redox governs energy, corrosion, and electronics. Both are transfer reactions — just transferring different particles.

When you harness redox reactions to produce or use electricity in a controlled way, that's electrochemistry — the science behind every battery, fuel cell, and electroplating process. It's the practical engineering of electron transfer, and it's next.

items.label: Charging your phone
items.hints: Electricity forces lithium ions to move back to the anode — reversing the redox reaction. Rechargeable batteries run redox both directions.

items.label: Browning apple
items.hints: Phenol compounds in the apple are OXIDIZED by O₂. Lemon juice (antioxidant) slows this by being oxidized preferentially.

items.label: Burning gas stove
items.hints: CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O. Carbon oxidized (−4→+4). Oxygen reduced (0→−2). Combustion is always redox.

items.label: Silver tarnish
items.hints: Ag + H₂S → Ag₂S. Silver oxidized (0→+1). Sulfur reduced (−2 stays, but H goes from +1 to 0 in H₂). The black tarnish is silver sulfide.

blocks.body: Next: electrochemistry — batteries, electrolysis, and harnessing electron flow for practical power.

blocks.title: Redox: The Universal Reaction
blocks.body: Breathing? Redox. Eating? Redox. Rusting car? Redox. Phone battery? Redox. Photosynthesis? Redox. Bleaching stains? Redox. About 80% of everything interesting in chemistry involves electron transfer.

Photosynthesis and cellular respiration are the SAME reaction running in opposite directions. Plants reduce CO₂ to glucose (storing energy). Animals oxidize glucose back to CO₂ (releasing energy). The entire biosphere is one giant redox cycle with the sun as the energy input. Life is a battery that runs on sunlight.
Next: electrochemistry — where we take redox reactions and put them in a box with wires. Also known as 'batteries.' Also known as 'the reason modern civilization works.'

questions.question: In cellular respiration (C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O), what is oxidized and what is reduced?

options.A: Glucose is reduced, oxygen is oxidized
options.B: Glucose is oxidized (carbon loses electrons), oxygen is reduced (gains electrons)
options.C: Nothing is oxidized — this is acid-base
options.D: Water is oxidized, CO₂ is reduced

questions.explanation: Carbon in glucose: average oxidation state 0. In CO₂: +4. Carbon lost electrons → glucose oxidized. Oxygen: 0 in O₂ → −2 in CO₂/H₂O. Gained electrons → oxygen reduced. This redox reaction powers all aerobic life.

questions.question: Photosynthesis is the reverse of respiration. If respiration oxidizes glucose, what does photosynthesis do?

options.B: Reduces CO₂ back into glucose — the reverse electron transfer, powered by sunlight
options.D: Decomposes water into hydrogen and oxygen only

questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Carbon goes from +4 (CO₂) to ~0 (glucose) — REDUCED. Water is oxidized (O goes from −2 to 0 in O₂). Sunlight provides the energy to drive this 'uphill' redox.
